past_medical_history: Traumatic head injury at age 22 in a bus-motorcycle collision, hospitalized in ICU for several days without loss of consciousness at scene
Difficulty walking during recovery from head injury (age 22), evaluated with EMG which was normal at that time
Diagnosed with posttraumatic epilepsy at age 22, four months after the accident
Treated sequentially with phenytoin, carbamazepine, and diazepam without seizure reduction over 26 years
Ten years of psychotherapy and antidepressant treatment in her late 20s and 30s without improvement in episodes
Major depressive disorder diagnosed at age 30

medications.frequency: As needed, up to three times daily

family_history: Mother with generalized anxiety disorder and migraines
Father with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Paternal uncle with epilepsy diagnosed in childhood, well-controlled on medication
One brother (age 52) with no neurological conditions
Maternal grandmother with dementia (onset in her 80s)

social_history.occupation: Former seamstress, currently unemployed for 3 years due to recurrent seizure-like episodes
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband and one adult daughter (age 24) in a small apartment. Husband works as a construction laborer. Patient relies on family members to accompany her outside the home due to fear of having episodes in public.
social_history.exercise: Minimal physical activity due to fear of triggering episodes. Previously active before symptom onset.
social_history.psychosocial_stressors: History of the traumatic motor vehicle accident at age 22 that preceded the onset of all symptoms. Ongoing financial strain due to unemployment and medication costs. Social isolation secondary to fear of episodes in public. Patient reports feeling that her life was derailed by the accident and the subsequent diagnosis of epilepsy.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect is anxious but cooperative. Speech is fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Attention intact on digit span (6 forward, 4 backward). Memory 3/3 registration, 3/3 recall at 5 minutes. Clock drawing normal. Fund of knowledge appropriate for education level. No thought disorder. Patient appears distressed when discussing her episodes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. Upper extremities: strength 5/5 bilaterally in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors/flexors, grip, and intrinsic hand muscles. No pronator drift. Lower extremities: strength 5/5 bilaterally in hip flexors, knee extensors/flexors, ankle dorsiflexors, ankle plantarflexors. No atrophy. No fasciculations. No involuntary movements at rest.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, proprioception, and vibration intact throughout all extremities. No dermatomal or peripheral nerve distribution sensory loss. Double simultaneous stimulation normal.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. 2+ and symmetric at patellae and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus. No Hoffman sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal in upper and lower extremities.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait with appropriate stride length and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No ataxia.
neurological_exam.additional: Neurological examination is entirely normal between episodes. No focal deficits. No signs of lateralizing pathology. The normal interictal examination in the context of 26 years of refractory 'seizures' occurring 2-3 times per week is noteworthy.

patient.chief_complaint: Refractory seizures occurring 2-3 times per week for 26 years, unresponsive to multiple antiepileptic medications
patient.history_present_illness:
Mrs. Ramirez is a 48-year-old right-handed woman referred for inpatient evaluation of medically refractory seizures. Her seizure history dates back 26 years, beginning at age 22, approximately four months after she sustained a traumatic head injury in a bus-motorcycle collision. At the time of the accident, she did not lose consciousness but was hospitalized in the intensive care unit for several days. During her recovery, she experienced difficulty walking and underwent an electromyogram, which was reportedly normal. Four months later, she began experiencing paroxysmal behavioral and motor episodes occurring two to three times per week.

Her episodes are described as two distinct types. The first type consists of transient periods of unresponsiveness lasting several seconds to a few minutes, during which she appears to stare blankly and does not respond to verbal commands; her family refers to these as her 'absences.' The second type involves more dramatic motor manifestations: she describes a sensation of distress or agitation, followed by jerking movements of her trunk and extremities, bizarre posturing, leg shaking, spitting, and crying. These motor episodes typically last approximately one minute. After these events, she often points to her head saying it hurts but is able to converse with those around her and does not appear confused afterward. Her family reports that she seems aware during the motor episodes and can sometimes describe what happened immediately after.

She was diagnosed with posttraumatic epilepsy shortly after the episodes began and has been treated sequentially and in combination with phenytoin, carbamazepine, and diazepam. Despite adequate doses and good compliance, there has been no reduction in episode frequency over 26 years. She also underwent approximately ten years of psychotherapy and received antidepressant treatment, neither of which reduced the frequency of her episodes. She has been seen by multiple neurologists over the years, and the episodes have been attributed to refractory posttraumatic epilepsy. She is now referred for continuous video-EEG monitoring to characterize her episodes and guide further management. The patient is frustrated and frightened, expressing concern that her condition is worsening and that she will never be able to live a normal life. She is also concerned about the long-term effects of taking multiple antiepileptic drugs for decades.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz well-organized alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Drowsiness achieved with vertex waves; stage II sleep with K-complexes and sleep spindles noted

findings.state: Wakefulness, drowsiness, and stage II sleep

findings.location: Bilateral temporal regions, left slightly more prominent than right
findings.morphology: Intermittent runs of rhythmic theta activity, lasting 2-5 seconds

artifacts.location: Bilateral temporal and frontal leads intermittently

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed. No change in background. No epileptiform discharges induced. No clinical symptoms provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 8-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Interictal EEG is predominantly normal with intermittent bitemporal theta slowing that is nonspecific and may reflect medication effect or drowsiness. No epileptiform discharges identified despite prolonged recording. No clinical events were captured during this initial recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: No habitual clinical events were captured during this initial 4-hour routine recording. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is necessary to characterize the patient's typical episodes and determine whether they have an electrographic correlate.

findings.location: Right anterior temporal pole, cortical and subcortical

additional_observations: No mesial temporal sclerosis bilaterally — hippocampal volumes and signal are symmetric and normal
No cortical dysplasia or other epileptogenic structural lesion
Right maxillary sinus mucosal thickening is an incidental finding, likely chronic sinusitis
mri.impression: No mesial temporal sclerosis or other structural epileptogenic lesion. No mass, demyelination, or acute intracranial process. The encephalomalacia could theoretically serve as an epileptogenic focus, but interictal SPECT (if performed) and EEG correlation are needed to determine clinical significance. Normal brain MRI otherwise. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No acute or chronic abnormalities. Normal ECG.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: GGT 62 U/L (H), Vitamin D (25-OH) 18 ng/mL (L).

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and sleep spindles. All sleep stages recorded. No interictal epileptiform discharges during sleep.

findings.type: Clinical event captured - motor episode type 1 (major event)
findings.frequency: Three events captured over 5 days (durations: 55 seconds, 70 seconds, 65 seconds)
findings.morphology: During all three events, the EEG showed persistent normal waking alpha rhythm with superimposed muscle artifact. No ictal pattern, no rhythmic evolution, no post-ictal slowing. Video review shows events beginning with jerking of the trunk, progressing to bizarre bilateral limb movements, leg shaking, spitting, and crying. Patient pointed to her head stating it hurt. Eyes open during events. Patient responded to questions during events and had no post-event confusion.

findings.type: Clinical event captured - unresponsive staring episode (absence-like)
findings.morphology: During both events, normal waking EEG background persisted. No 3 Hz spike-and-wave or other generalized epileptiform pattern. No focal rhythmic activity. Video shows patient staring with eyes open, not responding to verbal commands, but with normal EEG. During the second event, when her name was called loudly, she briefly startled before resuming the unresponsive state.
Brief runs of rhythmic theta, 2-4 seconds, identical to initial recording

artifacts.impact: Moderate artifact during major motor events, but underlying cerebral activity clearly identifiable throughout all events due to persistent posterior dominant rhythm visible in posterior leads

output.impression: Normal 5-day continuous video-EEG monitoring with AED taper. Three habitual major motor events and two absence-like staring episodes captured, all five without electrographic correlate. The EEG demonstrated normal background activity during all events. No interictal epileptiform discharges were identified during the entire 5-day recording including wakefulness, drowsiness, and all stages of sleep. The intermittent bitemporal theta slowing is a nonspecific finding likely related to medications. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: None significant. Adequate number of habitual events captured during monitored AED taper. AEDs were tapered during monitoring (carbamazepine tapered to 200 mg daily, phenytoin to 100 mg daily, diazepam to 5 mg daily) to facilitate event capture, with no emergence of epileptiform activity during taper.

Nerve_Conduction_Studies.reference_range: Motor: DL <4.4ms, amp >4.0mV. Sensory: amp >20 microV

additional_observations: No evidence of myelopathy at any level
No demyelinating lesions in the spinal cord
Normal conus medullaris termination at L1
output.impression: Normal cervical and thoracic spinal cord MRI with contrast. No evidence of myelopathy, demyelinating disease, or structural cord lesion. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Processing Speed Index 86 standard score (L), Beck Depression Inventory-II 26 raw score (H), Beck Anxiety Inventory 28 raw score (H).

Psychiatric_Evaluation.value: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) with attacks or seizures (PNES); Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent, moderate; Generalized Anxiety Disorder; Features of PTSD related to the motor vehicle accident

Psychiatric_Evaluation.reference_range: 0-4 none, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: DSM-5 Diagnostic Assessment Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) with attacks or seizures (PNES); Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent, moderate; Generalized Anxiety Disorder; Features of PTSD related to the motor vehicle accident  (H), PHQ-9 (Depression screening) 16 score (H), GAD-7 (Anxiety screening) 15 score (H), PCL-5 (PTSD screening) 38 score (H), Dissociative Experiences Scale (DES) 25 score (H).

output.query: posttraumatic epilepsy psychogenic nonepileptic seizures differential diagnosis video EEG

results.title: Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures after head injury: a population-based study
results.summary: PNES occurs in approximately 10-20% of patients referred to epilepsy monitoring units after traumatic brain injury. Head injury is a common antecedent to PNES, and the two conditions (posttraumatic epilepsy and PNES) can coexist, requiring video-EEG for differentiation.

results.title: Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures: review and update
results.summary: PNES accounts for 20-30% of patients referred for refractory epilepsy evaluation. Mean delay to diagnosis is 7-10 years. Video-EEG is the gold standard for diagnosis. Key features distinguishing PNES from epileptic seizures include: retained awareness, non-stereotyped movements, gradual onset and offset, side-to-side head movements, pelvic thrusting, eye closure during events, and absence of post-ictal confusion.

results.title: Post-event serum prolactin levels in the differentiation of epileptic seizures from PNES
results.summary: Serum prolactin levels measured 15-20 minutes after an event are typically elevated >2x baseline after generalized tonic-clonic and complex partial seizures but remain normal after PNES. Sensitivity is moderate (50-60% for GTC) and specificity is high (>90%). It is a useful adjunctive test but not diagnostic alone.

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.source: Espay et al., The Lancet Neurology 2018;17(1):84-98
results.summary: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs rather than by exclusion. PNES is a subtype of FND. The diagnosis carries the same diagnostic certainty as other neurological conditions when positive signs are present. Treatment involves psychotherapy, physiotherapy, and clear communication of the diagnosis.

output.summary: Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (PNES) are a common differential for posttraumatic epilepsy, occurring in 10-20% of TBI patients referred for epilepsy evaluation. Mean diagnostic delay is 7-10 years. Video-EEG is the gold standard for diagnosis. Key distinguishing features of PNES include retained awareness, bizarre non-stereotyped movements, eye closure, absence of post-ictal confusion, and no EEG correlate during events. Post-event prolactin is a useful adjunctive test (normal in PNES, elevated after epileptic seizures). PNES is classified as a subtype of functional neurological disorder. Treatment involves psychotherapy and clear diagnostic communication.

output.proposed: Gradual discontinuation of carbamazepine, phenytoin, and diazepam in a patient with PNES (not epilepsy)
interactions: Carbamazepine is a potent CYP3A4 inducer — discontinuation will increase sertraline levels (sertraline is CYP3A4 substrate). Monitor for serotonergic side effects and consider sertraline dose reduction.
Phenytoin is a CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 inducer — discontinuation will similarly increase sertraline levels.

Diazepam discontinuation after years of chronic use requires slow taper (reduce by no more than 10-25% every 2-4 weeks) to avoid benzodiazepine withdrawal syndrome, which can include seizures, anxiety, insomnia, and rarely delirium.
contraindications: Abrupt discontinuation of diazepam after chronic use is contraindicated due to risk of withdrawal seizures and potentially life-threatening withdrawal syndrome
warnings: Even though the patient has PNES rather than epilepsy, abrupt AED withdrawal could theoretically lower seizure threshold. Given the small right temporal encephalomalacia from prior TBI, there is a theoretical (though unproven) risk of provoked seizures if AEDs are stopped abruptly.
Withdrawal from carbamazepine should also be gradual to avoid rebound symptoms.
Monitor vitamin D levels after AED discontinuation — levels may normalize without enzyme-inducing AEDs.
alternatives: If sertraline dose adjustment is needed after AED taper, consider switching to an SNRI (venlafaxine, duloxetine) which may also help with any comorbid pain or anxiety
For anxiety management during benzodiazepine taper, consider hydroxyzine, buspirone, or gabapentin as bridging agents

signal_characteristics.perfusion: Symmetric perfusion throughout bilateral cerebral hemispheres
signal_characteristics.temporal_lobes: Symmetric temporal lobe perfusion bilaterally, no focal hypoperfusion

additional_observations: No focal area of hypoperfusion or hyperperfusion
The small right anterior temporal encephalomalacia noted on MRI does not demonstrate a corresponding significant perfusion abnormality
output.impression: Normal interictal SPECT study with symmetric cerebral perfusion. No focal perfusion abnormality to suggest an active epileptogenic focus. The right anterior temporal encephalomalacia does not show significant corresponding perfusion deficit. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder - psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) with dissociative episodes, misdiagnosed as posttraumatic epilepsy for 26 years

differential.likelihood: Low - initially the working diagnosis for 26 years given the temporal relationship between TBI and seizure onset. However, video-EEG demonstrating no electrographic correlate during five captured habitual events, normal interictal EEG without epileptiform discharges, normal interictal SPECT, and normal post-event prolactin collectively exclude epilepsy. The small right temporal encephalomalacia is an insufficient lesion to explain the clinical presentation.
differential.key_features: Would expect epileptiform discharges on interictal EEG, ictal EEG changes during events, post-ictal confusion, elevated post-event prolactin, stereotyped semiology. None present.

differential.likelihood: Very low - approximately 10-20% of PNES patients also have epilepsy. However, no epileptiform discharges were seen during 5 days of continuous monitoring including AED taper, and no events had EEG correlates. The right temporal encephalomalacia is small and anterior, not in the typical mesial temporal location for TLE. Normal interictal SPECT further argues against an active epileptogenic focus.
differential.key_features: Would require at least some events with EEG correlate or interictal epileptiform discharges. Neither was found.

differential.likelihood: Low - frontal lobe seizures can have bizarre motor manifestations and may have minimal scalp EEG correlate. However, the retained awareness, non-stereotyped nature, variable duration, and absence of any EEG changes across five captured events make this diagnosis extremely unlikely.
differential.key_features: Frontal seizures are typically brief (<30s), highly stereotyped, may arise from sleep, and usually show some EEG correlate with prolonged monitoring. This patient's events do not match.

differential.likelihood: Very low - normal ECG, events do not involve loss of consciousness with typical presyncopal symptoms, and the motor manifestations are not consistent with convulsive syncope.
differential.key_features: Would expect ECG abnormalities, hemodynamic changes during events, brief loss of consciousness. Not present.

differential.likelihood: Very low - patient passes symptom validity testing (TOMM 49/50), demonstrates genuine distress, has not sought secondary gain, and has been functionally impaired for 26 years. The distinction between FND (involuntary symptoms) and malingering (deliberate fabrication) is important.
differential.key_features: Would expect failure on symptom validity testing, evidence of external incentives, inconsistency in reporting. Patient passes validity testing and is genuinely distressed.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive history including detailed characterization of seizure-like episodes (semiology, frequency, duration, awareness, post-event state), temporal relationship to head injury, response to antiepileptic medications, and psychosocial context
optimal_actions.expected_finding: 26-year history of refractory episodes despite therapeutic AED levels, two distinct event types (motor episodes with retained awareness and no post-ictal confusion, and dissociative staring episodes), onset 4 months after TBI — raising suspicion for non-epileptic events

optimal_actions.action: Review prior workup and medication history, noting therapeutic AED levels with continued high seizure frequency as a red flag for non-epileptic events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Therapeutic carbamazepine and phenytoin levels despite 2-3 episodes per week for 26 years is highly suggestive that episodes are not epileptic seizures

optimal_actions.action: Order initial routine EEG to assess for interictal epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal interictal EEG with nonspecific bitemporal theta slowing (likely medication effect), no epileptiform discharges. No events captured. Findings insufficient to confirm or exclude epilepsy — prolonged monitoring needed.

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for structural epileptogenic lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Small right anterior temporal encephalomalacia from remote TBI — a potential red herring that could support the epilepsy diagnosis but is insufficient to explain the clinical presentation. No mesial temporal sclerosis.

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, AED levels, and baseline prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs except mild GGT elevation (AED effect) and low vitamin D (AED effect). Therapeutic AED levels confirming compliance despite continued events. Normal baseline prolactin for future comparison.

optimal_actions.action: Proceed with prolonged continuous video-EEG monitoring (3-5 days) with AED taper to capture habitual events and determine whether they have an electrographic correlate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Five habitual events captured (three major motor episodes and two staring episodes), all without electrographic correlate, diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and dissociative episodes. No epileptiform discharges during entire 5-day recording even during AED taper.

optimal_actions.action: Diagnose psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) as a subtype of functional neurological disorder based on video-EEG evidence and positive clinical features (retained awareness, non-stereotyped movements, no post-ictal confusion)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Definitive diagnosis of PNES, reclassifying 26 years of presumed posttraumatic epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Communicate diagnosis to patient clearly and empathically, explaining that her episodes are real neurological events but not epileptic seizures, and that effective treatments exist
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understands that PNES is a recognized neurological condition, not fabricated symptoms, and that antiepileptic medications are not the appropriate treatment

optimal_actions.action: Plan gradual tapering and discontinuation of unnecessary antiepileptic medications (carbamazepine, phenytoin) and careful benzodiazepine taper, with appropriate drug interaction monitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identify that AED discontinuation will affect sertraline metabolism and that diazepam taper must be slow to prevent withdrawal

optimal_actions.action: Refer for psychotherapy (CBT for PNES, trauma-focused therapy for PTSD related to MVA) and psychiatric optimization of depression and anxiety treatment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initiation of evidence-based treatment for PNES including psychotherapy and psychiatric care

critical_actions: Perform prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events and determine whether they have an electrographic correlate — this is the gold standard diagnostic test for differentiating epileptic seizures from PNES
Recognize that therapeutic AED levels with continued high seizure frequency for 26 years is a major red flag for non-epileptic events, not simply refractory epilepsy
Do not be anchored by the 26-year diagnosis of posttraumatic epilepsy or the small temporal encephalomalacia on MRI — these do not confirm that the episodes are epileptic

Diagnose PNES based on positive video-EEG evidence (events without electrographic correlate) combined with clinical features (retained awareness, no post-ictal confusion, non-stereotyped movements), not merely by exclusion
Communicate the diagnosis with empathy, validating that symptoms are real, explaining the mechanism, and providing hope for improvement with appropriate treatment
Plan gradual discontinuation of unnecessary AEDs, with particular attention to slow benzodiazepine taper to avoid withdrawal
Address the underlying psychiatric comorbidity (depression, anxiety, PTSD) that is maintaining the condition
contraindicated_actions: Adding more antiepileptic drugs or increasing current AED doses for presumed refractory epilepsy without first performing video-EEG to confirm the episodes are epileptic
Attributing the right temporal encephalomalacia as definitive proof of posttraumatic epilepsy without EEG confirmation that events arise from this region
Abruptly discontinuing diazepam after years of chronic use — this risks benzodiazepine withdrawal seizures
Telling the patient that her episodes are fake, imagined, or that nothing is wrong — this is harmful and clinically incorrect
Suggesting the patient is malingering — she passes symptom validity testing and is genuinely distressed
Performing epilepsy surgery evaluation or resective surgery based on the temporal encephalomalacia without evidence of epileptic seizures
Starting additional CNS-active medications without reviewing the current polypharmacy burden
Dismissing the psychiatric comorbidity (depression, anxiety, PTSD) as secondary or unimportant — these are maintaining factors for PNES
key_reasoning_points: The key diagnostic puzzle in this case is that the patient has a plausible organic explanation for epilepsy (post-TBI onset, right temporal encephalomalacia on MRI) that anchors clinicians toward the epilepsy diagnosis for 26 years, making PNES the unexpected finding.

Therapeutic AED levels with continued high seizure frequency is one of the strongest clinical red flags for PNES. A patient having 2-3 seizures per week on three AEDs at therapeutic levels for 26 years should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis.

The bitemporal theta slowing on interictal EEG is a nonspecific finding (likely from medications) that could be misinterpreted as supporting an epilepsy diagnosis, adding to the diagnostic misdirection.

The right anterior temporal encephalomalacia from remote TBI is a significant red herring — it provides a plausible structural substrate for posttraumatic epilepsy, but video-EEG proves the events are non-epileptic.

Video-EEG monitoring is the gold standard for diagnosing PNES. The absence of EEG correlate during five captured habitual events (including both event types) is diagnostic, especially given the bilateral motor manifestations that would invariably produce EEG changes if epileptic.
The family history of a paternal uncle with epilepsy is another anchoring red herring that could reinforce the epilepsy diagnosis.

The preserved awareness during motor events, absence of post-ictal confusion, ability to respond to questions during events, and non-stereotyped bizarre movements are all positive clinical features of PNES.

Post-event prolactin remaining at baseline (14.1 vs 12.4 ng/mL) after a captured motor event supports non-epileptic etiology, as generalized tonic-clonic and complex partial epileptic seizures typically elevate prolactin >2x baseline.

The temporal relationship between the traumatic MVA and symptom onset, combined with PTSD features, moderate depression, severe anxiety, and elevated dissociative experiences, illustrates the biopsychosocial model of PNES development.

Iatrogenic harm from 26 years of misdiagnosis includes unnecessary AED polytherapy with metabolic consequences (low vitamin D, elevated GGT), chronic benzodiazepine dependence, social disability (unemployment, agoraphobia-like avoidance), and adoption of a chronic illness identity.

Gradual AED discontinuation is both therapeutic and logistically complex — particularly the benzodiazepine taper, which requires careful management to avoid withdrawal seizures (which would be true epileptic seizures from withdrawal, not PNES).

metadata.condition_name: Functional neurological disorder - Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES)
metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to plausible organic explanation (post-TBI onset, temporal encephalomalacia on MRI, family history of epilepsy), 26-year diagnostic anchoring to posttraumatic epilepsy, nonspecific EEG findings that could support epilepsy, and the need for prolonged video-EEG to capture events and prove non-epileptic etiology
metadata.sub_domain: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures with posttraumatic anchoring
metadata.estimated_complexity: Diagnostic puzzle - 26-year misdiagnosis of posttraumatic epilepsy, structural MRI lesion as red herring, requires video-EEG for definitive diagnosis
red_herrings: Right anterior temporal encephalomalacia from remote TBI providing plausible structural basis for epilepsy
Paternal uncle with epilepsy suggesting genetic predisposition
Bitemporal theta slowing on interictal EEG that could be misinterpreted as supporting temporal lobe epilepsy
26-year history of diagnosed posttraumatic epilepsy creating strong anchoring bias
Mildly elevated GGT from chronic AED use could distract from the central question of whether the episodes are epileptic
Low vitamin D from chronic enzyme-inducing AED use is a real finding but unrelated to the seizure-like episodes
